'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|qq=|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|qq=|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. '''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

}}

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. '''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.

In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>

Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

'''Kotoshironushi no Kami''' ({{ill|Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8009611}}) is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Descriptions in Historical Texts ==
The deity is also referred to as Yaegotoshironushi no Kami and Yaekotoshironushi no Kami. According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}.

During the {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja|葦原中国平定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni Heitei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国譲り|1=Kuni-yuzuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621119}} (the subjugation of Ashihara no Nakatsukuni), when [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] demanded that Ōkuninushi hand over his lands, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision to his son, Kotoshironushi. Takemikazuchi then went to {{ill|Miho no Seki|ja|美保ヶ崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miho no Seki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where Kotoshironushi agreed to relinquish the land. Following this, Kotoshironushi sank into the sea, symbolizing his retreat from worldly affairs.

== Genealogy ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Kotoshironushi is the child of [[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja|神屋楯比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Tatehime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神屋楯比売|1=Kamuyatate hime no mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30935372}}. In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], the mother is listed as {{ill|Takatsuhime no Kami|ja|高津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuhime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Kotoshironushi's offspring include:
- {{ill|Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja|櫛御方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushimikata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鴨王 (上古)|1=Kamo no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55809532}}
- {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja|媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime Tatara Isuzuhime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒメタタライスズヒメ|1=Himetataraisuzuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353793}}, the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
- {{ill|Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja|五十鈴依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzu Yorihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十鈴依媛命|1=Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10881557}}

== Worship ==
Kotoshironushi is enshrined in various shrines across Japan, including [[:en:Miho Shrine|Miho Shrine]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagata Shrine|Nagata Shrine]] in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], and {{ill|Kamo Tsuba Shrine|ja|鴨都波神社|1=Kamo Tsuba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Tsuba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The deity is also celebrated at shrines dedicated to [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], such as {{ill|Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|ja|京都ゑびす神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|1=Kyoto Ebisu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248501}} and [[:en:Imamiya Ebisu Shrine|Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]].

== Legends ==
=== The Rooster Legend ===
In [[:en:Mihonoseki, Shimane|Mihonoseki]], a legend states that Kotoshironushi despises roosters. According to the tale, Kotoshironushi visited {{ill|Mihozuhime|ja|美保津姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihozuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三穂津姫|1=Mihotsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6378698}} nightly. One night, a rooster crowed prematurely, startling him, and he accidentally left his paddle behind. This led to an encounter with a crocodile (or shark), which bit his hand, causing him to resent roosters thereafter. This legend influences local customs, prohibiting the keeping of roosters or the consumption of eggs.

=== Association with Izu ===
In the modern period, Kotoshironushi is sometimes conflated with the deity of [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]] in the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]], where he is worshiped alongside {{ill|Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} as a god of island creation and fishing.

{{Nihongo|'''Kotoshironushi'''|事代主神}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Yae Kotoshironushi no kami'''|八重言代主神}}, is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]''.
In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', Kotoshironushi is the son of [[Ōkuninushi]], the earthly deity of [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]]. When the heavenly deities sent [[Takemikazuchi]] to conquer Izumo, Ōkuninushi deferred the decision over whether to resist to his two sons. Kotoshironushi, who had been fishing at the time of Takemikizuchi's arrival, agreed to accept the rule of the heavenly gods, surrendered his spear and left Izumo.<ref name="Kidder2007">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Edward Kidder|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&pg=PA116|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|page=116}}</ref> His brother [[Takeminakata]] fought with Takemikazuchi and was defeated.<ref name="Hackin1932">{{cite book|author=J. Hackin|title=Asiatic Mythology: A Detailed Description and Explanation of the Mythologies of All the Great Nations of Asia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAZrFhvqnTkC&pg=PA395|year=1932|publisher=Asian Educational Services|isbn=978-81-206-0920-4|page=395}}</ref>
Kotoshironushi is the principal deity of the [[Asuka Shrine|Asuka shrine]],<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book|author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA499|date=3 June 2014|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|pages=499–500}}</ref> and is associated with the god [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]].<ref name="HavensInoue2006">{{cite book|author1=Norman Havens|author2=Nobutaka Inoue|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ|year=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|page=88}}</ref> In mythology, he was an adviser to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] during her invasion of [[:en:Korea|Korea]]. He was also one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court.<ref name="Aston2013">{{cite book|author=William Aston|title=Shinto (The Way of the Gods)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pOZRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT172|date=11 December 2013|publisher=Adegi Graphics LLC|isbn=978-0-543-01408-5|page=172}}</ref> His daughter [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Atsushi|first1=Kadoya|title=Kotoshironushi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=95|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=14 September 2015}}</ref>

no Miyatsuko|ja|長国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakuni no Miyatsuko|1=Nagakuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21653903}}, {{ill|Tosa no
